comments.text: Some exact values of h, for reference. Writing h(m) for the maximum over 1 ≤ k < m of the least number of distinct divisors of m summing to k (computed exactly by DP, cross-checked with two independent implementations): h(n!) for n = 3,...,11: 2, 3, 4, 5, 5, 6, 7, 7, 7. Among practical m ≤ 16384 the largest with h(m) = 4 is m = 180, and the minimum of h over each dyadic block [2^j, 2^{j+1}) equals 5 for 8 ≤ j ≤ 13 (attained at m = 264, 528, 1050, 2100, 4200, 9240). Also h(720720) = 6, h(4324320) = 7.

When formalizing, The Formal Conjectures Authors noticed an issue that needs to be documented: The original description is ambigous in that it doesn't clarify whether the same subset should be applied to all numbers < n! . However, it only makes sense if for every $m\lt n!$ a new subset is taken.

You are indeed allowed to use all divisors of $n!$. So to fully spell out the second question: does there, for every $\epsilon > 0$, exist an $n_0$ such that for every $n \ge n_0$ and for every $m < n!$ there is a set $S_{m,n}$ such that 1) For all $i \in S_{m,n}$ we have that $i$ divides $n!$. 2) The sum $\sum_{i \in S_{m,n}} i$ is equal to $m$. 3) The size $|S_{m,n}|$ is smaller than $n^{\epsilon}$.
Perhaps it should be added which of these questions has the prize attached to it; namely, the first one. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I think it's actually a little unclear - he just writes 'for a proof or disproof of'\[h(n) < (\log\log n)^{O(1)},\]which makes no sense as written without further quantifiers. Based on the context I think he meant this to be about $h(n!)$ (and then $\log\log n$ should become $\log n$) and this was just a typo.

It's true that 'for a proof or disproof of $h(n) < (\log \log n)^C$' makes no sense without quantifiers, but the words before this inequality are 'for infinitely many $n$'. So I read this as 'for a proof or disproof of [the existence of infinitely many $n$ with] $h(n) < (\log \log n)^C$'. I therefore do not think it's about $h(n!)$ specifically. Edit 3-2-2026: reading it again, I agree with Dogmachine and my past self, so I pressed the update button. Somewhat amusingly, as Erdős defined $h(n) = 0$ in [Er81h] if $n$ is not practical, the inequality $h(n) < (\log \log n)^C$ trivially holds for infinitely many $n$. But I'm afraid I don't get to claim \$250 quite this easily. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
